message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
i want to build a prompt data base to store my prompts for future prompting, how could i structure this?

addedBlocks:
## claude.ai Context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- socialmedia: Social media strategy session with the Social Media Strategist — platform strategy, community, thought leadership
- content: Content strategy session with the Content Creator — editorial planning, copy, brand storytelling
- brand: Brand strategy session with the Brand Guardian — identity, positioning, consistency
- invest: Investment analysis session with the Investment Advisor — portfolio strategy, equity analysis, personal finance
- seo: SEO strategy session with the SEO Specialist — technical SEO, content strategy, organic growth
- growth: Growth strategy session with the Growth Hacker — acquisition, funnels, viral loops, experimentation
- health: Health and wellness session with the Health Coach — nutrition, fitness, sleep, longevity, lab results
- consult: Open a strategy session with a senior Big Four consultant who challenges your ideas and brainstorms rigorously
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Two options, given you're already using an Obsidian vault:

**Option A — Vault-native (recommended):** Create `MindFactory/Prompts/` with one file per prompt category. Prompts live alongside your knowledge, get wikilinked to related concepts, and are searchable with Obsidian's search. Tradeoff: not queryable by a script/API.

**Option B — Structured file store:** A `prompts/` directory with YAML frontmatter (tags, model, purpose, quality rating) and markdown body. Easy to grep, pipe into scripts, or load into a Claude session. Tradeoff: disconnected from your vault.

Given your vault architecture, I'd lean toward **Option A inside your vault** with a schema like:

```
MindFactory/Prompts/
  ├── _index.md          ← master list with ratings
  ├── research.md        ← research prompts
  ├── writing.md         ← writing/editing prompts
  ├── coding.md          ← dev/debugging prompts
  └── analysis.md        ← synthesis/analysis prompts
```

Each prompt entry:
```yaml
---
prompt_id: p-001
tags: [research, synthesis]
model: claude-sonnet-4-6
quality: ⭐⭐⭐⭐
last_used: 2026-05-01
---
## [Prompt Name]
[prompt text]
**Use when:** ...
**Avoid when:** ...
```

Want me to build this structure out in your vault? I can also add a template to `02_Templates/` for new prompt entries.

message.content: hm why not in TechNotes a new Category Called AI then under it?

content.text:
Good call — `TechNotes/05_AI/` fits better. TechNotes is your technical knowledge library, and prompts are technical artifacts. It also keeps them alongside any future AI/LLM notes (model comparisons, API patterns, agent workflows).

```
TechNotes/05_AI/
  ├── Prompts/
  │   ├── _index.md
  │   ├── research.md
  │   ├── writing.md
  │   ├── coding.md
  │   └── analysis.md
  └── (future: Models.md, APIs.md, Agent_Patterns.md, etc.)
```

Want me to build this out?
Let me explore the existing TechNotes structure first.

input.prompt:
Explore the TechNotes directory at /Users/rezna/symbiose/TechNotes/ and the 02_Templates directory at /Users/rezna/symbiose/02_Templates/. I need to understand:
1. The folder structure and naming conventions used in TechNotes (existing categories like 01_Fundamentals, 02_CyberSec, etc.)
2. A sample of existing note files to understand frontmatter schema (tags, status, domain fields)
3. Any templates in 02_Templates that might apply to new knowledge pages
4. What the _index or MOC files look like if any exist

Report exact file paths, frontmatter fields used, and any consistent conventions.

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have all the information needed. Let me compile the comprehensive report.

## Comprehensive Exploration Report: TechNotes & Templates

### 1. TechNotes Folder Structure & Naming Conventions

**Top-Level Categories (4 main domains):**
- `01_Fundamentals/` — foundational knowledge (networking, operating systems)
- `02_CyberSec/` — security-focused content (defensive, offensive, GRC, vulnerability management, cryptography)
- `03_Cloud_Devops/` — cloud and DevOps technologies (containerization, IaC, observability, VCS, cloud platforms, storage)
- `04_Certifications/` — certification prep materials (organized by domain: Linux, CyberSec, Cloud)

**Key Structural Patterns:**
- **Numeric prefixes** (01_, 02_, 03_, 04_) organize hierarchical categories by priority
- **Subcategories use underscores** (e.g., `01_Defensive`, `02_Offensive`, `03_GRC` under CyberSec)
- **Further nesting** for larger domains (e.g., `cloud_platforms/azure/services/`)
- **Supporting directories**: `pics/` folders for images/diagrams; `resources/` for external references
- **Total markdown files**: 122 notes across all categories
- **File naming**: Lowercase with hyphens or underscores (e.g., `security_controls.md`, `web_application_attacks.md`)

**Existing Directory Examples:**
- `/TechNotes/02_CyberSec/01_Defensive/` — digital_forensics, endpoint_security, incident_response, SIEM_SOC, threat_intel, RE_&_malware_analysis
- `/TechNotes/03_Cloud_Devops/` — cloud_platforms (azure), containerization, iac, observability, storage&backup, vcs
- `/TechNotes/04_Certifications/Cloud/AWS/` — Cloud_Practitioner, Solution_Architect_Associate
- `/TechNotes/04_Certifications/CyberSec/COMPTIA/Sec+/`, `/CyberSec/CySa+/`

---

### 2. Frontmatter Schema & Fields

**Standard Frontmatter Format (YAML):**

```yaml
---
tags:
  - domain/[category]     # Required: domain/security, domain/devops, domain/networking, domain/linux, domain/windows, domain/cloud
  - type/[type]           # Required: type/concept, type/tool, type/protocol, type/service, type/certification
  - source/[source]       # Optional: source/sec+, source/lfcs, source/work, source/htb, source/self
  - status/[status]       # Optional: status/seedling, status/growing, status/evergreen
created: YYYY-MM-DD       # Optional: often blank in newer files
---
```

**Observed Tag Combinations:**

| File Type | Domain Tags | Type Tags | Example |
|-----------|------------|-----------|---------|
| **Concept notes** | `domain/security` or `domain/devops` | `type/concept` | security_controls.md, web_application_attacks.md |
| **Tool notes** | `domain/devops`, `domain/linux` | `type/tool` | docker.md, kubernetes.md |
| **Certification MOCs** | `domain/security`, `domain/linux` | `type/certification` | Sec+.md, LFCS.md |
| **Networking** | `domain/networking` | `type/concept` | general.md, firewalls.md |

**Special Frontmatter Variants:**

*CVE Template:*
```yaml
---
cve: CVE-xxx-xxx
title: "{{title}}"
impact: "{{impact}}"
why: "{{why}}"
source: "{{link}}"
---
```

*Blog Post Template:*
```yaml
---
title: "{{title}}"
date: { date:YYYY-MM-DD }
description:
---
```

*Article Template:*
```yaml
---
title: "{{title}}"
author: ""
url: ""
date: { date:YYYY-MM-DD }
summary:
keyinsights:
whyitmatters:
---
```

*Challenges Template:*
```yaml
---
domain:
Status:
quartal:
---
```

*Books Template:*
```yaml
---
Author: 
Title: 
Date: <% tp.date.now("YYYY-MM-DD-dddd") %>
Status: Reading
---
```

---

### 3. Content Structure & Consistent Conventions

**Standard Note Layout (TemplateTopic.md):**
1. **Title** — H1 heading
2. **One-liner** — Abstract blockquote: `> [!abstract] One-liner [description]`
3. **What is it** — Plain language explanation (not copied from docs)
4. **How it works** — Mechanisms, diagrams, ASCII art
5. **Key concepts / Components** — Tables comparing multiple items
6. **Commands / Configuration** — Code blocks with bash examples + [!tip] admonitions
7. **Attack surface / How it gets abused** — Vulnerabilities, exploitation vectors, CVE links
8. **Defense / Hardening** — Security controls and mitigation strategies
9. **Related** — Wiki-style backlinks `[[note_name]]`

**Certification MOC Layout (TemplateCertMOC.md):**
1. **Exam Overview** — Code, domains, questions, duration, passing score, cost (table format)
2. **Domains** — Numbered domains with percentages, linked notes, unmapped topics
3. **Cert-Specific Notes** — Exam tips, mnemonics, practice questions only
4. **Resources** — Course links, practice exams, official objectives
5. **Related** — Links to other certification MOCs

**Actual MOC Example: Azure MOC** (`/TechNotes/03_Cloud_Devops/cloud_platforms/azure/Azure MOC.md`)
- Maps subcategories (Core & Infrastructure, Security, DevOps & Automation) to note links
- Uses `[[Azure Virtual Machines]]` wiki-style reference format

---

### 4. Templates Available in 02_Templates/

**Location:** `/Users/rezna/symbiose/02_Templates/`

| Template File | Purpose | Key Fields |
|---------------|---------|-----------|
| **TemplateTopic.md** | Knowledge page for concepts, tools, protocols | tags (domain, type, source, status), created, H1 title, abstract, What/How/Components/Commands/Attack/Defense/Related sections |
| **TemplateCertMOC.md** | Certification master index | tags (domain, type/certification), Exam Overview table, Domains with section percentages, Cert-Specific Notes, Resources |
| **TemplateCVE.md** | CVE tracking | cve, title, impact, why, source |
| **TemplateBlogpost.md** | Blog post ideation/drafting | title, date, description, Hook/Gap/Unique Angle/Confidence/Raw Notes sections |
| **TemplateArticles.md** | Article summary & notes | title, author, url, date, summary, keyinsights, whyitmatters |
| **TemplateBooks.md** | Book reading tracker | Author, Title, Date, Status (Reading) |
| **TemplateChallenges.md** | Learning goal/challenge tracker | domain, Status, quartal, Goal, Related Notes |
| **TemplateTimeless.md** | Quote/inspiration logging | Author, Date, Quote |
| **TemplateNewsletter.md** | Newsletter aggregator (dataviewjs) | Uses queries to pull recent blogposts, articles, CVEs |

**Template Application:** The `TemplateTopic.md` is the primary template for new TechNotes knowledge pages.

---

### 5. Index & MOC Files

**Only MOC Found:**
- **File Path:** `/Users/rezna/symbiose/TechNotes/03_Cloud_Devops/cloud_platforms/azure/Azure MOC.md`
- **Structure:** Category-based (Core & Infrastructure, Security, DevOps & Automation) with wiki-style links `[[note_name]]`
- **Frontmatter:** Standard (tags with domain/cloud + type/concept, created date)
- **No global root index** exists — TechNotes reliance appears to be on folder structure navigation

**Certification MOCs exist at subdomain level:**
- `/TechNotes/04_Certifications/CyberSec/COMPTIA/Sec+/Sec+.md` (links to 50+ child notes)
- `/TechNotes/04_Certifications/Linux/LFCS/LFCS.md`
- Follow the `TemplateCertMOC.md` pattern with Exam Overview tables and Domain sections

---

### 6. Key Conventions & Best Practices

1. **Tag Structure is Hierarchical:**
   - `domain/*` — primary knowledge domain (mandatory)
   - `type/*` — content type/format (mandatory)
   - `source/*` — where knowledge came from (optional but recommended)
   - `status/*` — note maturity level (optional, seedling→growing→evergreen)

2. **Naming Conventions:**
   - Files: lowercase, hyphens or underscores (e.g., `web_application_attacks.md`)
   - Categories: numeric prefix + underscore + capitalized name (e.g., `02_CyberSec`)

3. **Content Quality Signals:**
   - H1 title required
   - One-liner abstract using `[!abstract]` blockquote
   - Tables for comparisons (CIA triad, control categories, attack types)
   - Code blocks with practical commands
   - [!tip] admonitions for gotchas
   - Wiki-style backlinks `[[related_note]]` for internal navigation

4. **File Organization:**
   - Organize by **domain first** (security, devops, networking)
   - Then by **subdomain** (defensive/offensive under security)
   - Create `pics/` folders co-located with notes for diagrams
   - Create `resources/` folders for external references

5. **Frontmatter Maturity:**
   - Most notes have `created` dates (often blank in recent files)
   - No explicit `status` field used yet in existing notes, but `TemplateTopic.md` defines `status/seedling|growing|evergreen`
   - Optional `source/` tags not yet heavily used in existing notes

---

### Summary

The TechNotes knowledge base is a **domain-partitioned, tag-indexed personal knowledge base** with:
- **4 top-level categories** (Fundamentals, CyberSec, Cloud/DevOps, Certifications) using numeric prioritization
- **122 markdown notes** with consistent YAML frontmatter (domain, type, optionally source/status)
- **Standard content template** (What/How/Components/Commands/Attack/Defense sections)
- **MOC pattern** for certification domains (Sec+, LFCS, Azure)
- **9 reusable templates** in `/02_Templates/` for different content types (knowledge pages, certifications, blogs, articles, CVEs, books, challenges, timeless quotes)
- **Wiki-style linking** using `[[note_name]]` for cross-references
- **Image support** via co-located `pics/` subdirectories

All exact file paths, frontmatter fields, and conventions have been documented above for your reference when creating new knowledge pages.

input.content:
# Plan: TechNotes/05_AI Prompt Database

## Context
User wants a prompt database inside their Obsidian vault. Rather than MindFactory, `TechNotes/05_AI/` fits better — prompts are technical artifacts, and the AI category can grow to include model notes, API patterns, and agent workflows.

## Structure to Create

```
TechNotes/05_AI/
  Prompts/
```

# AI Prompts Index

> [!abstract] Prompt database — categorized, rated, and ready to reuse.

| Category | File | Count |
|----------|------|-------|
| Research & Synthesis | [[research]] | 0 |
| Writing & Editing | [[writing]] | 0 |
| Coding & Debugging | [[coding]] | 0 |
| Analysis & Comparison | [[analysis]] | 0 |
| System & Meta | [[system]] | 0 |

## How to use this database

- Each file groups prompts by use case
- Entries include model, quality rating, and last-used date
- Update **Count** above and **Last used** in the entry when you reuse a prompt
- Quality ratings reflect real-world results — adjust as you learn

## Related

- [[TechNotes/05_AI/Prompts/research|research]]
- [[TechNotes/05_AI/Prompts/writing|writing]]
- [[TechNotes/05_AI/Prompts/coding|coding]]
- [[TechNotes/05_AI/Prompts/analysis|analysis]]
- [[TechNotes/05_AI/Prompts/system|system]]
